title: Pakistan Melts Amid Political Instability And Economic Chaos | Heatwave Tests Resolve
cleaned_description: Temperatures soared to over 50 degrees Celsius (122 degrees Fahrenheit) in parts of Pakistan on Monday, as an ongoing heat wave gripped more areas, including the southern district of Sukkur. The heat wave is forecast to continue for at least a few more days. Doctors are treating victims of heatstroke at hospitals across Pakistan. Heatstroke relief camps manned by volunteers have been set up to provide iced water for people in Sukkur, with residents saying the government has done nothing to help them. People have also taken to the waters of the popular Indus River to save themselves from the extreme heat. Javed Ahmed said he and his friends came to the river every day, as electricity supply cuts meant they couldn't keep cool in their homes. Authorities have urged people to stay indoors, hydrate and avoid unnecessary travel. But labourers say they need to work to feed their families. Portrait was commissioned to celebrate Charles' 50 years as a member of the Drapersâ€™ Company. Drapers' Company was set up over 600 years ago as a trade association for wool merchants. title: Iconic Mosque On UNESCO 'World Heritage In Danger' List Re-Plastered By Residents | Key Details
cleaned_description: Iconic Mosque On UNESCO 'World Heritage In Danger' List Re-Plastered By Residents | Key Details  Annual re-plastering of the Great Mosque of Djenne in the West African country of Mali. An important step in maintaining the integrity of the worldâ€™s largest mud-brick building. Great Mosque of Djenne has been on UNESCOâ€™s World Heritage in Danger list since 2016. Djenne is a historical centre of Islamic learning & sister city to the better-known Timbuktu. Northern Mali was taken over by rebels in 2012, who were pushed back by French forces. An insurgency took hold in Maliâ€™s north and moved into the centre & south of the nation. Maliâ€™s main military camp, just 15 km from Bamako, was attacked by Islamist militants in 2022. The same year, Djenneâ€™s annual plastering was nearly cancelled entirely due to unrest. Djenneâ€™s mosque requires a new layer of mud each year before the start of the rainy season. Without a new layer of mud each year, the iconic building will fall into disrepair. Traditionally, males are responsible for climbing the mosque and putting the new layer of mud. Women and girls are responsible for fetching water from the nearby river to mix with clay. Djenneâ€™s tourism industry has all but completely disappeared due to the ongoing insecurity. cleaned_description: Democratic Arizona Gov. Katie Hobbs has signed a bill to repeal a ban on most abortions.  The action Thursday relegates the 1864 law approved before Arizona became a state to the past. The repeal wonâ€™t go into effect until 90 days after the legislative session ends, which typically is June or July.  Hobbs says signing the bill is just the beginning of a fight to protect reproductive health care in Arizona. It won final legislative approval Wednesday in the Arizona Senate, with two GOP lawmakers joining Democrats.  A repeal means that a 2022 statute banning the procedure after 15 weeks of pregnancy would become Arizonaâ€™s prevailing abortion law.  A crowd of lawmakers â€” mostly women â€” joined the governor at the signing ceremony. Some were gripped by emotion as they cheered the repeal and said more is needed to protect rights to reproductive health care.  Former Democratic state Rep. Athena Salman was visibly emotion while she celebrated approval of the repeal she initially proposed in 2019.  â€œI canâ€™t stop thinking about my daughters ... that future generations will not have to live under the restrictions and the interference that weâ€™ve had to experience," said Salman, who resigned from the legislature in January to lead an abortion rights group. title: Georgian Police Deploy Tear Gas As Mass Protests Over 'Russian Law' Continue In Tbilisi
cleaned_description: Protesters took to the streets of Tbilisi on Wednesday evening as Georgia's Parliament moved a step closer to passing a law that critics fear will stifle media freedom and endanger the country's European Union membership bid.  Georgian police were seen using water cannons, tear gas and pepper spray against the tens of thousands of protesters who thronged surrounding streets.  Dozens of people were arrested the night before, and mass rallies continue daily in the capital, Tbilisi.  Protesters denounce the bill as â€œthe Russian lawâ€ because neighbouring Russia uses similar legislation to stigmatise independent news media and organisations critical of the Kremlin.  The law would require media and noncommercial organisations to register as â€œpursuing the interests of a foreign power,â€ if they receive more than 20% of funding from abroad.  The ruling Georgian Dream party withdrew a similar proposal last year after large crowds protested.  Eighty-three of Georgiaâ€™s 150 lawmakers approved the bill in its second reading, while 23 voted against it.  A third and final vote in Parliament is needed before it can be signed into law.  Georgian lawmaker Irakli Kobakhidze told reporters on Wednesday that he expected that final vote to happen in mid-May.          Republic TV is India's no.1 English news channel since its launch.
